request.question: Which genus of plant had more species identified with it in the past, Carissa or Aruncus?

title: Carissa
Aruncus
Synodontis omias
Aruncus dioicus
Tephritid Workers Database
IUCN Red List extinct in the wild species
Ornithogalum lebaense
Verrucomicrobia
Lancefield grouping
Platanthera azorica
sentences: Carissa is a genus of shrubs or small trees native to tropical and subtropical regions of Africa, Australia and Asia.
Until recently about 100 species were listed, but most of them have been relegated to the status of synonyms or assigned to other genera, such as "Acokanthera".
Aruncus is a genus of clump forming herbaceous perennial plants in the family Rosaceae.
Botanical opinion of the number of species differs, with from one to four species accepted.
They are closely related to the genera "Filipendula" and "Spiraea", and are native to mountainous damp woodland in temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere.
Creamy white plumes of flowers are produced above veined and toothed leaflets.
Synodontis omias is a species of upside-down catfish native to the Niger River basin of Guinea, Mali, Niger and Nigeria.
It was first described by German-born British zoologist, ichthyologist, and herpetologist Albert Günther in 1864, from a specimen collected in Jebba, Nigeria, on the Niger River.
There have been very few specimens of this species identified, and it may be the same species as "S. budgetti" from the Upper Niger.
Aruncus dioicus (Walt.)
(known as goat's beard, buck's-beard or bride's feathers) is a flowering herbaceous perennial plant in the family Rosaceae, and is the type species of the genus "Aruncus".
It has alternate, pinnately compound leaves, on thin, stiff stems, with plumes of feathery white or cream flowers borne in summer.
This plant can be found in moist woodland, often at higher altitudes, throughout temperate areas of Europe, Asia, and eastern and western North America.
In the UK it is considered suitable for planting in and around water areas, and has gained the Royal Horticultural Society's Award of Garden Merit.
The Tephritid Workers Database is a web-based database for sharing information on tephritid fruit flies.

Because these species are one of the most economically important group of insect species that threaten fruit and vegetable production and trade worldwide, a tremendous amount of information is made available each year: new technologies developed, new information on their biology and ecology; new control methods made available, new species identified, new outbreaks recorded and new operational control programmes launched.
The TWD allows workers to keep up-to-date on the most recent developments and provides an easily accessible and always available resource.
Version 2014.2 of the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species identified 78 (39 animal, 39 plant) extinct in the wild species, subspecies and varieties, stocks and subpopulations.
Ornithogalum lebaense is a flowering plant that grows in southwest Angola.
It was discovered on January 2009 by Ernst Jacobus van Jaarsveld.
The word ""lebaense"" refers to Leba Pass, where the plant lives.
"O. lebaense" does not have a known common name, most likely because its habitat is not one that is easily accessible by local people.
There are three other "Ornithogalum" species identified in Angola: "Ornithogalum tenuifolium", "O. pulchrum" and "O. benguellens".
Verrucomicrobia is a phylum of bacteria.
This phylum contains only a few described species ("Verrucomicrobium spinosum", is an example, the phylum is named after this).
The species identified have been isolated from fresh water and soil environments and human faeces.

A number of as-yet uncultivated species have been identified in association with eukaryotic hosts including extrusive explosive ectosymbionts of protists and endosymbionts of nematodes residing in their gametes.
While verrucae is another name for the warts often found on hands and feet, this phylum is so called not because it is a causative agent thereof, but because of its wart-like morphology.
Lancefield grouping is a method of grouping catalase-negative, coagulase-negative bacteria based on the carbohydrate composition of bacterial antigens found on their cell walls.

The system, created by Rebecca Lancefield, was historically used to organize the various members of the family Streptococcaceae, which includes the genera Lactococcus and Streptococcus, but now is largely superfluous due to explosive growth in the number of streptococcal species identified since the 1970s.
As of 2016, Lancefield designations are often used to communicate medical microbiological results in the United States.

Enterococcus, formerly known as Group D Streptococcus, was believed to be a member of the genus Streptococcus until 1984, after the Lancefield criteria were devised, and so were included in the original Lancefield grouping.
Many - but not all - species of streptococcus are beta-hemolytic.
Notably, Enterococcus and Streptococcus bovis (Lancefield Group D) are not beta-hemolytic.

Though there are many groups of streptococcus, only five are known to commonly cause disease in immune-competent human beings: Group A, Group B, both members of Group D, and two groups that lack the Lancefield carbohydrate antigen: Streptococcus pneumoniae and Viridans streptococci.
Platanthera azorica, commonly known as Hochstetter's butterfly orchid, is a species of orchid in the genus "Platanthera".
It was "rediscovered" in 2013 on a single volcanic ridge on the central Azores island of São Jorge, and "is arguably Europe's rarest "bona fide" orchid species."

While "P. azorica" was previously considered by some sources to be the same as "Platanthera micrantha", the 2013 discovery triggered a thorough analysis of historic and current data and specimens, resulting in three species identified.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Which genus of plant had more species identified with it in the past, Carissa or Aruncus?
hop1_docs: Aruncus dioicus | Aruncus dioicus (Walt.)  (known as goat's beard, buck's-beard or bride's feathers) is a flowering herbaceous perennial plant in the family Rosaceae, and is the type species of the genus "Aruncus".  It has alternate, pinnately compound leaves, on thin, stiff stems, with plumes of feathery white or cream flowers borne in summer.  This plant can be found in moist woodland, often at higher altitudes, throughout temperate areas of Europe, Asia, and eastern and western North America.  In the UK it is considered suitable for planting in and around water areas, and has gained the Royal Horticultural Society's Award of Garden Merit.

Aruncus | Aruncus is a genus of clump forming herbaceous perennial plants in the family Rosaceae.  Botanical opinion of the number of species differs, with from one to four species accepted.  They are closely related to the genera "Filipendula" and "Spiraea", and are native to mountainous damp woodland in temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere.  Creamy white plumes of flowers are produced above veined and toothed leaflets.

Binomial nomenclature | Binomial nomenclature (also called binominal nomenclature or binary nomenclature) is a formal system of naming species of living things by giving each a name composed of two parts, both of which use Latin grammatical forms, although they can be based on words from other languages.  Such a name is called a binomial name (which may be shortened to just "binomial"), a binomen, binominal name or a scientific name; more informally it is also called a Latin name.  The first part of the name identifies the genus to which the species belongs; the second part identifies the species within the genus.  For example, humans belong to the genus "Homo" and within this genus to the species "Homo sapiens".  The "formal" introduction of this system of naming species is credited to Carl Linnaeus, effectively beginning with his work "Species Plantarum" in 1753.  But Gaspard Bauhin, in as early as 1623, had introduced in his book "Pinax theatri botanici" (English, "Illustrated exposition of plants") many names of genera that were later adopted by Linnaeus.

Cyrtandra (plant) | Cyrtandra is a genus of flowering plants containing about 600 species, with more being discovered often, and is thus the largest genus in the family Gesneriaceae.  These plants are native to Southeast Asia, Australia, and the Pacific Islands, with the centre of diversity in Southeast Asia and the Malesian region.  The genus is common, but many species within it are very rare, localized, endangered endemic plants.  The species can be difficult to identify because they are highly polymorphic and because they readily hybridize with each other.  The plants may be small herbs, vines, shrubs, epiphytes, or trees.  The genus is characterized in part by having two stamens, and most species have white flowers, with a few red-, orange-, yellow- and pink-flowered species known.  Almost all species live in rainforest habitats.

Securinega | Securinega is a genus of plants in the family Phyllanthaceae, first described as a genus in 1789.  As presently conceived, the genus is native to Madagascar and the Mascarene Islands in the Indian Ocean.  In the past, it was considered to be much more widespread, thus explaining the long list of species formerly included.

Carissa | Carissa is a genus of shrubs or small trees native to tropical and subtropical regions of Africa, Australia and Asia.  Until recently about 100 species were listed, but most of them have been relegated to the status of synonyms or assigned to other genera, such as "Acokanthera".

Machlomovirus | Machlomovirus is a genus of plant viruses, in the family Tombusviridae.  Plants serve as natural hosts.  There is currently only one species in this genus: the type species "Maize chlorotic mottle virus" (MCMV), which causes significant losses in maize production worldwide.  MCMV was first identified in the U.S. state of Kansas causing corn lethal necrosis, a severe disease that negatively affects all stages of development for maize plants.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Aruncus — [2] "Botanical opinion of the number of species differs, with from one to four species accepted."
- Aruncus dioicus — [1] "type species of the genus \"Aruncus\"."
- Carissa — [6] "Until recently about 100 species were listed."

Ambiguities:
- Aruncus: species count is a range "from one to four species accepted" (variation in accepted species).
- Carissa: count is approximate ("about 100") and timeframe unspecified ("Until recently").

Bridge / missing fact:
- The key relation (historical number of species associated with each genus) is present and comparable in the passages, so no additional fact is required to answer the question. Final conclusion: Carissa had more species identified with it in the past (~100 vs. 1–4 for Aruncus).

Retrieval direction:
- "Carissa" (genus) page — confirm historical species list and timing of reclassifications.
- "Aruncus" (genus) page — confirm current accepted species count and historical treatments.
- Query: "Carissa number of species until recently" for sources on the ~100 figure.
- Query: "Aruncus number of species accepted 1 to 4" for sources detailing the accepted range.
hop2_docs: Nematus spiraeae | Nematus spiraeae is a species of sawfly in the family Tenthredinidae, known as the aruncus sawfly and sometimes the spiraea sawfly.  It is native to central and northern Europe and was first recorded in Britain in 1924.  Its larvae feed on the leaves of goat's beard ("Aruncus dioicus").

Aruncus | Aruncus is a genus of clump forming herbaceous perennial plants in the family Rosaceae.  Botanical opinion of the number of species differs, with from one to four species accepted.  They are closely related to the genera "Filipendula" and "Spiraea", and are native to mountainous damp woodland in temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere.  Creamy white plumes of flowers are produced above veined and toothed leaflets.

Aruncus dioicus | Aruncus dioicus (Walt.)  (known as goat's beard, buck's-beard or bride's feathers) is a flowering herbaceous perennial plant in the family Rosaceae, and is the type species of the genus "Aruncus".  It has alternate, pinnately compound leaves, on thin, stiff stems, with plumes of feathery white or cream flowers borne in summer.  This plant can be found in moist woodland, often at higher altitudes, throughout temperate areas of Europe, Asia, and eastern and western North America.  In the UK it is considered suitable for planting in and around water areas, and has gained the Royal Horticultural Society's Award of Garden Merit.
Sophronica carissae | Sophronica carissae is a species of beetle in the family Cerambycidae.  It was described by Fisher in 1930.

Carissa | Carissa is a genus of shrubs or small trees native to tropical and subtropical regions of Africa, Australia and Asia.  Until recently about 100 species were listed, but most of them have been relegated to the status of synonyms or assigned to other genera, such as "Acokanthera".
Carissa Capobianco | Carissa Capobianco (born July 5, 1988) is an American actress.

Carissa (Spain) | Carisa or Carissa (Greek: Κάρισσα , Ptol.  ii.  4, § 13) was an ancient city of the Turdetani in Hispania Baetica, in the "conventus" of Gades, having the "civitas Latina".  Several of its coins are extant.  Pliny refers to the city as Carissa "cognomine" Aurelia.  The city is usually associated, due to inscriptions, with Carixa, near Bornos, in the Province of Cadiz.
response.summary_2: Carissa had about 100 species listed until recently, while Aruncus has only one to four accepted species, so Carissa had more species identified with it in the past.
